heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK Subscribe acknowledgement -> 3.9.3 SUBACK Payload
sentence: In the MQTT v5 protocol, when a server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet, it MUST respond with a SUBACK packet containing a list of Reason Codes that correspond one-to-one with the Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order. The Reason Code value 0x00 ("Granted QoS 0") indicates that the subscription request for the associated Topic Filter has been accepted, but the server will deliver messages to this subscription with a maximum QoS level of 0, regardless of any higher QoS level requested by the client. This rule applies during the subscription acknowledgment phase and ensures that clients understand the actual QoS level they will receive, which may be lower than requested due to server policies or limitations.

sentence: When a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet containing one or more topic filters, the server MUST respond with a SUBACK packet containing a corresponding list of reason codes. heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK Subscribe acknowledgement -> 3.9.3 SUBACK Payload
sentence: The SUBACK packet MUST include a Reason Code value of 135 (Hex 0x87) with the name 'Not authorized' when the Server determines that the Client lacks the necessary permissions to subscribe to a specific topic filter. This authorization failure applies during the subscription processing phase after receiving a valid SUBSCRIBE packet and before sending the SUBACK response. When this Reason Code is returned for a topic filter, the subscription for that specific topic is rejected and the Client will not receive any messages published to that topic. The Server MUST apply this Reason Code in the SUBACK payload at the position corresponding to the unauthorized topic filter in the original SUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order as the requested subscriptions. This authorization check typically occurs after successful authentication but before granting subscription permissions, and may be based on the Client's identity, security credentials, or configured access control policies.

sentence: When a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet containing topic filters with wildcard characters (such as '+' or '#'), and the server does not support wildcard subscriptions, the server MUST include reason code 162 (0xA2) with the name 'Wildcard Subscriptions not supported' in the SUBACK payload for each affected topic filter. This reason code applies specifically to subscription requests containing wildcards and indicates that the server has rejected the subscription due to its policy of not supporting wildcard pattern matching. The reason code MUST appear in the SUBACK payload in the same position as the corresponding wildcard topic filter in the original SUBSCRIBE packet. When this rule is followed, the client will know that the specific subscription using wildcards was not accepted, while other non-wildcard subscriptions in the same packet may still be processed normally. This rule applies only when the server receives a valid SUBSCRIBE packet containing wildcard topic filters and the server's configuration explicitly prohibits wildcard subscriptions.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK Subscribe acknowledgement -> 3.9.3 SUBACK Payload
sentence: When a client attempts to subscribe to a shared subscription topic filter (prefixed with "$share/") and the server does not support shared subscriptions for that client, the server MUST include reason code 158 (0x9E) in the SUBACK payload for that specific topic filter. This reason code applies only when the client's SUBSCRIBE packet contains a valid shared subscription topic filter but the server either lacks shared subscription capability entirely or has disabled it for this particular client. The server MUST maintain the order of reason codes to match the corresponding topic filters in the original SUBSCRIBE packet. When this reason code is returned, the shared subscription request is rejected and no messages will be delivered through that shared subscription channel, though other non-shared subscriptions in the same SUBSCRIBE packet may still be processed normally.

sentence: When a server responds to a SUBSCRIBE packet with a SUBACK packet, it MUST include exactly one Subscribe Reason Code for each Topic Filter that was present in the original SUBSCRIBE request. heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK Subscribe acknowledgement -> 3.9.3 SUBACK Payload
sentence: When a server processes a SUBSCRIBE packet containing multiple topic filters, it must generate a SUBACK packet where the payload contains exactly one reason code for each topic filter in the original SUBSCRIBE packet, maintaining the same order. Even when an error applies to all topic filters in the packet (such as Packet Identifier in use), the server must still include the same reason code for every topic filter, preserving the positional matching requirement. This rule ensures that clients can accurately map each subscription outcome to its corresponding topic filter request.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.11 UNSUBACK Unsubscribe acknowledgement -> 3.11.3 UNSUBACK Payload
sentence: The UNSUBACK packet payload MUST contain a list of Reason Codes that correspond one-to-one with the Topic Filters in the UNSUBSCRIBE packet being acknowledged, maintaining identical order [MQTT-3.11.3-1]. heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.11 UNSUBACK Unsubscribe acknowledgement -> 3.11.3 UNSUBACK Payload

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.2 CONNACK Connect acknowledgement -> 3.2.2 CONNACK Variable Header -> 3.2.2.3 CONNACK Properties -> 3.2.2.3.15 Response Information
sentence: The Response Information property (identifier 0x1A) in the CONNACK packet contains a UTF-8 Encoded String that serves as the foundation for constructing a Response Topic. This property applies when the Client includes a Request Response Information property with value 1 in its CONNECT packet, though the Server may optionally omit it even when requested. The specification does not mandate how the Client transforms this string into a Response Topic, but it must be unique to the Client's session and typically forms the root of a topic tree where both publishing and subscribing Clients require authorization. The Response Information enables centralized Server configuration for request/response patterns, eliminating per-Client configuration. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error, and its implementation depends on proper Server configuration to ensure topic namespace uniqueness and access control.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: In the MQTT v5 DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) must appear at most once. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies specifically when a client is sending a DISCONNECT packet and attempts to define multiple Session Expiry Interval values. When this violation occurs, the receiving server must treat the packet as malformed and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13. Note that this rule applies regardless of the Session Expiry Interval value set in the initial CONNECT packet, and servers are additionally prohibited from including this property in DISCONNECT packets altogether.
final_prompt:
You are provided with a set of descriptions extracted from the MQTT v5 protocol specification:
In the MQTT v5 DISCONNECT packet variable header properties, the Session Expiry Interval property (identifier 0x11) must appear at most once. Including this property multiple times constitutes a Protocol Error. This rule applies specifically when a client is sending a DISCONNECT packet and attempts to define multiple Session Expiry Interval values. When this violation occurs, the receiving server must treat the packet as malformed and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13. Note that this rule applies regardless of the Session Expiry Interval value set in the initial CONNECT packet, and servers are additionally prohibited from including this property in DISCONNECT packets altogether.

Your task is to analyze the meaning of each rule in these descriptions and determine whether it falls within our scope of interest.

Follow this analysis process step-by-step for each rule:
(1)The rule describes a specific MQTT v5 protocol message type or its internal fields, and this message type can be sent or received by an implementation of the protocol.
(2)The rule explicitly describes or implies, through field value ranges, format definitions, or construction/processing procedures, the logic that the protocol program should apply when generating (sending) or processing (receiving) such a message.
This logic must be detailed enough to directly guide a developer’s implementation.
(3)The described message logic pertains to handling a single message instance — including atomic or transactional operations for constructing or validating its variable bindings or fields — or verifying their consistency against previous messages in the same protocol exchange.
Logic involving concurrency with other messages or external events is excluded.

For each rule:

1.If all three criteria (1), (2), and (3) are satisfied, respond with "Conforms."
2.If any criterion is not met, respond with "Does not conform."

Please omit all explanatory content.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: When processing a DISCONNECT packet, if the Session Expiry Interval property is not present in the packet, the Session Expiry Interval value previously established in the CONNECT packet must be used instead. This rule applies specifically to client-initiated DISCONNECT packets where session state management is relevant. The expected outcome is that the session expiry timing continues to be governed by the original CONNECT value, maintaining session persistence according to the client's initial configuration. This behavior is conditional upon the session not being explicitly set to non-persistent (Session Expiry Interval of zero) in the original CONNECT, and servers must never include this property in their DISCONNECT responses.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: The Session Expiry Interval property MUST NOT be included in a DISCONNECT packet sent by the Server [MQTT-3.14.2-2]. This rule applies specifically when the Server is initiating the disconnection, as only the Client is permitted to specify session persistence preferences during disconnect. If a Server violates this requirement by including the Session Expiry Interval, it constitutes a protocol error. The expected behavior is that the Server's DISCONNECT packet will contain no Session Expiry Interval property, and any session expiry timing remains governed by the value previously established in the CONNECT packet or Client's DISCONNECT. This rule ensures proper role separation where session lifetime configuration is exclusively a Client responsibility.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: The Session Expiry Interval property in the DISCONNECT packet is identified by the byte value 17 (0x11), followed by a four-byte integer representing the interval in seconds. This property must not be included more than once, as doing so constitutes a Protocol Error. If the Session Expiry Interval is omitted from the DISCONNECT packet, the value from the original CONNECT packet is used. Importantly, servers MUST NOT include this property in DISCONNECT packets [MQTT-3.14.2-2]. Additionally, if the client's CONNECT packet specified a Session Expiry Interval of zero, the client must not set a non-zero value in the DISCONNECT packet. If a server receives such an invalid non-zero value, it treats the packet as invalid and responds with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error), as specified in section 4.13.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.15 AUTH Authentication exchange
sentence: The AUTH packet is used bidirectionally between Client and Server to facilitate extended authentication mechanisms like challenge/response authentication. This packet exchange occurs only after successful establishment of an initial connection where both parties have agreed upon a common authentication method. The rule applies specifically during the authentication phase of the connection lifecycle. For valid operation, the authentication method specified in the AUTH packet must exactly match the Authentication Method declared in the initial CONNECT packet; any mismatch constitutes a Protocol Error and requires termination of the connection.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: When a Server receives a DISCONNECT packet from a Client that contains a Session Expiry Interval property in violation of protocol rules—specifically if the Client previously established a session with a zero Session Expiry Interval in the CONNECT packet and now attempts to set a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in the DISCONNECT—the Server MUST treat this as a protocol error. In such cases, the Server MUST respond by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error) as specified in section 4.13, and MUST NOT process the invalid DISCONNECT as a normal session termination. This ensures that session state consistency is maintained and prevents clients from improperly modifying session expiry after connection establishment.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.2 DISCONNECT Properties -> 3.14.2.2.2 Session Expiry Interval
sentence: When a Client sends a DISCONNECT packet, the Session Expiry Interval property must maintain consistency with the initial CONNECT packet. Specifically, if the Client previously established the session with a Session Expiry Interval of zero (indicating immediate session termination upon disconnection), it is a Protocol Error to specify a non-zero Session Expiry Interval in the DISCONNECT packet. If violated, the Server must reject the DISCONNECT packet as invalid and respond with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x82 (Protocol Error), following the error handling procedures in section 4.13. The prerequisite for this rule is that the Client must have successfully connected with a Session Expiry Interval of zero in the CONNECT packet.

This rule applies when either party sends an AUTH packet during the connection lifecycle. A Protocol Error occurs if the Authentication Method specified in the AUTH packet does not exactly match the Authentication Method declared in the initial CONNECT packet. This ensures authentication continuity and prevents mismatched security mechanisms during the session.

This ensures authentication continuity and prevents mismatched security mechanisms during the session.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.15 AUTH Authentication exchange -> 3.15.2 AUTH Variable Header
sentence: The Variable Header of the AUTH Packet MUST contain the Authenticate Reason Code followed by the Properties field, in that order, when the packet is used for authentication exchange in MQTT v5. This structure applies to both client and server AUTH packets during extended authentication flows, such as re-authentication or continuation of a multi-step authentication process. The Authenticate Reason Code indicates the purpose of the packet (e.g., success, continue authentication), while the Properties field carries authentication-related metadata encoded according to the rules in section 2.2.2. This ordering ensures consistent parsing and interoperability between MQTT implementations.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.4 DISCONNECT Actions
sentence: Upon receiving a DISCONNECT packet, the receiving endpoint (whether client or server) should immediately close the underlying Network Connection, thereby terminating the MQTT session transport. This action applies regardless of the DISCONNECT packet's Reason Code and follows the sender's obligation to cease transmitting further control packets. The receiver must ensure proper cleanup of session resources, including discarding any Will Message if the Reason Code indicates normal disconnection (0x00 Success), before closing the connection to maintain protocol state consistency.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.4 DISCONNECT Actions
sentence: After a client or server sends a DISCONNECT packet, it must immediately cease sending any further MQTT Control Packets over the same Network Connection and must close the connection. This rule applies once the DISCONNECT packet has been transmitted, regardless of whether the connection is closed immediately by the sender. The expected outcome is a clean termination of the MQTT session without residual packet transmission, ensuring proper handling of Will Messages (e.g., discarding them if the Reason Code is 0x00) and allowing the receiver to close the connection upon receipt. Prerequisites include a valid, active MQTT connection and the successful transmission of the DISCONNECT packet.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.4 DISCONNECT Actions
sentence: When a client initiates a clean disconnection by sending a DISCONNECT packet with Reason Code 0x00 (Success), the server must recognize this as an intentional termination of the connection. Upon receiving such a packet, the server is required to discard any Will Message that was established during the initial connection setup (as defined in section 3.1.2.5) without publishing it. This rule applies specifically when the client provides a Success reason code, indicating a graceful shutdown rather than an unexpected disconnection. The expected outcome is that the Will Message is silently dropped, ensuring it is not triggered unnecessarily for planned disconnections. This behavior preserves the semantic meaning of Will Messages as being reserved for unplanned connection terminations.

This rule applies during the authentication exchange process when either the client or server sends an AUTH packet to continue or complete the authentication handshake. When this rule is followed, the AUTH packet consists only of the fixed header and variable header sections, with the payload length field set to zero. This requirement applies to all AUTH packets regardless of authentication stage, and any AUTH packet containing a payload must be treated as a protocol violation, potentially resulting in connection termination.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.15 AUTH Authentication exchange -> 3.15.1 AUTH Fixed Header
sentence: The AUTH packet Fixed Header governs the initial structure of authentication exchange packets in MQTT v5. This rule applies whenever an AUTH packet is constructed during enhanced authentication flows, such as when continuing or re-authenticating a session. The expected outcome is a properly formatted fixed header where byte 1 contains the packet type (15) with bits 3-0 all set to 0, and byte 2 contains the Variable Byte Integer encoding of the variable header length. Prerequisites include: the connection must be in a state supporting enhanced authentication, and the packet must follow successful CONNECT/CONNACK exchange with authentication method agreement. Any deviation in reserved bits must be treated as a malformed packet, requiring immediate connection termination as per [MQTT-3.15.1-1].

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.3 SUBSCRIBE Payload -> 3.8.3.1 Subscription Options
sentence: In the MQTT v5 SUBSCRIBE packet payload, bits 0 and 1 of each Subscription Options byte represent the Maximum QoS field, which specifies the highest Quality of Service level at which the Server is permitted to deliver Application Messages to the Client for that subscription. This rule applies when a Client sends a SUBSCRIBE request to establish or update subscriptions. The expected outcome is that the Server will honor the specified QoS ceiling when forwarding messages matching the subscribed topic filter. A prerequisite is that the SUBSCRIBE packet must be properly formatted and authenticated. Note that setting the Maximum QoS field to value 3 constitutes a Protocol Error, as this value is reserved and invalid for QoS specification.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.3 SUBSCRIBE Payload -> 3.8.3.1 Subscription Options
sentence: Bit 2 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload represents the No Local option. When this option is set to 1, the Server MUST NOT forward Application Messages to any connection with the same ClientID as the publishing connection, effectively preventing a client from receiving its own published messages on the subscribed topic. This rule applies during message forwarding after a successful subscription and is particularly useful for bridging scenarios where clients relay messages between servers. However, setting the No Local bit to 1 on a Shared Subscription constitutes a Protocol Error. The option requires that the subscription is successfully established and only affects message distribution to connections with matching ClientIDs, without impacting other subscription parameters or QoS handling.

sentence: Bit 3 of the Subscription Options byte in the SUBSCRIBE packet payload represents the Retain As Published (RAP) option, which governs how the RETAIN flag is handled when Application Messages are forwarded to the subscriber. When this option is set to 1, messages retain their original RETAIN flag value as published; when set to 0, forwarded messages have the RETAIN flag cleared to 0. This rule applies during message forwarding after subscription establishment and affects all subsequent Application Messages matching the subscription's Topic Filter. Note that retained messages sent when the subscription is initially established always have the RETAIN flag set to 1, regardless of this option's value. The option is particularly useful for bridging scenarios where message metadata must be preserved across MQTT server boundaries.

sentence: When a client establishes a subscription with the Retain As Published (RAP) option set to 1 (bit 3 of the Subscription Options byte), the Server MUST preserve the original RETAIN flag value from published Application Messages when forwarding them to the subscriber. This applies to all Application Messages delivered through this subscription after its establishment, regardless of whether they are live publications or retained messages. The RETAIN flag in forwarded messages will match exactly what was set by the publisher. This behavior is distinct from the Retain Handling option, which only controls retained message delivery during subscription establishment, and from the case where RAP is 0, which forces the RETAIN flag to 0 in all forwarded messages. This rule enables bridging scenarios where subscribers need to distinguish between retained and non-retained messages in the message flow.

sentence: The Retain Handling option (bits 4-5 of the Subscription Options byte) controls the delivery of retained messages when a subscription is first established. This option applies specifically during the initial subscription setup phase and determines whether the server should send existing retained messages matching the topic filter. The expected behavior depends on the configured value: 0 sends all retained messages immediately, 1 sends retained messages only for new subscriptions (not existing ones), and 2 prevents any retained message delivery at subscription time. This rule applies only when processing SUBSCRIBE packets and requires that the subscription topic filter matches existing retained messages; if no retained messages exist, all values behave identically. The option does not affect retained message handling after the subscription is active, and values of 3 constitute a protocol error.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.3 SUBSCRIBE Payload -> 3.8.3.1 Subscription Options
sentence: **Non-normative comment** The No Local and Retain As Published subscription options can be used to implement bridging scenarios where a Client acts as a bridge between MQTT servers. Specifically, when a Client subscribes with No Local set to 1, it prevents receiving messages it published itself, avoiding message loops in bridge configurations. When Retain As Published is set to 1, forwarded messages preserve their original RETAIN flag, enabling the bridge to maintain message retention semantics when forwarding to another Server. These options apply during the SUBSCRIBE operation when the Client intends to relay messages between different MQTT ecosystems. The expected outcome is proper bridge functionality without duplicate messages or altered retention behavior. Prerequisites include the Client having valid subscriptions and the Server supporting these subscription options.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows
sentence: When the Server acts as an intermediary forwarding Application Messages between clients, the QoS level used for outbound delivery to a receiving Client MAY differ from the QoS level of the original inbound message from the sending Client. This applies specifically during message distribution in publish/subscribe flows where the Server delivers the same Application Message to multiple subscribers. Each outbound delivery is treated independently, allowing the Server to use the highest QoS level supported by each client connection while respecting the original message's maximum QoS, if specified. The expected outcome is that message delivery QoS is optimized per client capability without requiring uniform QoS levels across all subscribers.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.1 QoS 0: At most once delivery

This requirement applies specifically to initial transmissions in QoS 0 flows and assumes the sender has a valid message payload and topic destination prepared for publication.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.1 Session State -> 4.1.1 Storing Session State

This requirement is conditional upon the session being established with a non-zero Session Expiry Interval and remains in effect even if storage limits are approached, though administrators may still intervene in exceptional circumstances.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards
sentence: In MQTT v5, when a Client creates a subscription, the Topic Filter MAY include special wildcard characters—specifically the single-level ('+') and multi-level ('#') wildcards—to enable subscribing to multiple topics matching the filter pattern simultaneously. This rule applies during the SUBSCRIBE packet processing, where the Server MUST interpret these wildcards to determine matching Topic Names for message routing. The expected outcome is that the Client receives all PUBLISH messages whose Topic Names match the wildcard-based filter. Prerequisites include: the Topic Filter MUST be valid per MQTT syntax rules, wildcards MUST only appear in Topic Filters (never in Topic Names used for publishing), and each wildcard MUST occupy an entire topic level between separators ("/").

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.5 Message receipt
sentence: A Client MUST be prepared to receive and acknowledge MQTT PUBLISH packets according to applicable QoS rules even during an ongoing UNSUBSCRIBE operation, as message delivery and subscription removal are asynchronous processes where the Server might continue to deliver messages that matched previous subscriptions before the UNSUBSCRIBE completes. This applies when a Client has sent an UNSUBACK packet but the Server has not yet fully processed the subscription removal, and the Client MUST still process these messages according to normal QoS acknowledgment requirements regardless of whether it chooses to handle the application payload.

sentence: The topic level separator ("/") is used to introduce hierarchical structure into the Topic Name by dividing it into multiple discrete topic levels. This structural organization enables the wildcard matching mechanism in Topic Filters during subscription operations, where wildcard characters ("+" and "#") can match multiple topic levels but are explicitly prohibited from appearing in published Topic Names. The rule applies whenever a Client publishes to a topic or subscribes using a Topic Filter, and ensures proper topic matching and message routing throughout the MQTT system.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.5 Message receipt
sentence: After a Server accepts an incoming Application Message (thereby taking ownership), it MUST store the message in the Session State of each Client that has a matching Subscription, as defined by the topic matching rules in Section 4.7. This rule applies once the Server has determined message routing and before delivery to eligible Clients, ensuring that messages are retained according to the session and QoS requirements. The Server MUST perform this step regardless of whether the Subscription was explicitly created by the Client or automatically assigned, and even if an UNSUBSCRIBE operation for the topic is in progress. Clients are subsequently required to acknowledge receipt according to the applicable QoS rules, irrespective of whether they process the message content. [MQTT-4.5.0-1]

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.1 Session State
sentence: During MQTT session establishment, both Client and Server must associate session state with the Client Identifier to properly implement QoS 1 and QoS 2 message delivery guarantees. This rule applies when a client connects with a persistent session (Clean Start = 0) and requires reliable message delivery. The expected outcome is that both endpoints maintain synchronized state tracking for in-flight messages, including: unacknowledged QoS 1/2 messages sent to the peer, pending message transmissions, client subscriptions, and QoS 2 messages received but not fully acknowledged. This state persists across network reconnections and lasts for the duration of the Session Expiry Interval, ensuring message delivery guarantees are maintained even through temporary disconnections.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.1 Session State

This state management is conditional on the Client having an active session with a valid Client Identifier and is essential for implementing QoS 1 and QoS 2 guarantees.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.1 Session State
sentence: The MQTT session duration is determined by the time elapsed since the most recent Network Connection was established, extended by the Session Expiry Interval specified in the CONNECT packet. This rule applies when a client reconnects with a previously existing session and governs how long the server must maintain session state (including subscriptions, in-flight messages, and will message) after the network connection ends. The session remains valid and can be resumed during this period, after which the server must discard all session state. This behavior requires that the client specified a non-zero Session Expiry Interval and that the session was not explicitly deleted by the client.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards
sentence: When constructing MQTT topic structures, wildcard characters (single-level '+' and multi-level '#') are permitted exclusively in Topic Filters used for subscriptions to match multiple topics simultaneously, but are strictly prohibited in Topic Names used for publishing messages. This rule applies during both topic validation when publishing messages and filter validation when creating subscriptions. Publishers MUST ensure Topic Names contain no wildcards, while subscribers MAY use wildcards only in Topic Filters. Violation by publishers results in protocol-level publication rejection, whereas malformed Topic Filters cause subscription failure. The rule is enforced during client topic processing before network transmission.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.1 Topic level separator
sentence: The forward slash ( / U+002F) serves as the topic level separator that defines the hierarchical structure of MQTT topic names and filters. This rule applies whenever topics are constructed for publishing messages or creating subscription filters, and governs how the protocol interprets topic hierarchy during message routing and subscription matching. When this rule is followed, topics are properly segmented into distinct levels, enabling correct wildcard matching (using '+' and '#') and hierarchical message distribution. Adjacent separators create valid zero-length topic levels, and the separator can appear in any position within a topic name or filter. This structural convention must be maintained consistently across all topic-related operations in the protocol.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.1 Topic level separator
sentence: The forward slash (/) serves as the topic level separator that structures topic names hierarchically in MQTT v5. This rule governs how the separator interacts with wildcard characters in topic filters during the subscription process. Specifically, when a subscribing client includes either the multi-level (#) or single-level (+) wildcard in a topic filter, the placement of topic level separators determines how the wildcards match against topic names. The separator must be used correctly to ensure proper scope matching: the multi-level wildcard must follow a separator and be the last character, while the single-level wildcard must be bounded by separators (or at the start/end). Adjacent separators create zero-length topic levels, which remain valid. This behavior applies when clients establish subscriptions and persists throughout message routing, ensuring published messages match subscriptions only when the separator-wildcard relationships align with the topic hierarchy.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.4 SUBSCRIBE Actions
sentence: During the processing of a SUBSCRIBE packet from a client in an active session, if the server identifies a Topic Filter that exactly matches an existing Non-shared Subscription's Topic Filter within the same session, the server MUST replace the existing subscription with a new subscription. This replacement occurs regardless of whether the Subscription Options differ from the original subscription. The server must ensure that no application messages are lost during this transition, and if the Retain Handling option is set to 0, any retained messages matching the Topic Filter must be resent. This rule applies specifically to Non-shared Subscriptions and does not affect Shared Subscriptions, which follow different handling procedures. The server must complete this replacement before sending the corresponding SUBACK response to the client.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.4 SUBSCRIBE Actions
sentence: When a Server receives a valid SUBSCRIBE packet from a Client, it MUST respond with a SUBACK packet containing the same Packet Identifier as the received SUBSCRIBE packet [MQTT-3.8.4-1]. This response applies regardless of whether the subscription requests are granted or denied, and the SUBACK MUST include a Reason Code for each Topic Filter/Subscription Option pair indicating the granted QoS level or failure reason. The Server may begin sending matching PUBLISH packets immediately upon receiving the SUBSCRIBE packet, without waiting to send the SUBACK response. For multiple Topic Filters in a single SUBSCRIBE packet, the Server MUST process them as sequential subscriptions but combine all responses into a single SUBACK packet [MQTT-3.8.4-5]. This rule applies during normal protocol operation when handling subscription requests and ensures reliable subscription state synchronization between Client and Server.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.4 SUBSCRIBE Actions
sentence: When a Client includes Subscription Identifiers in a SUBSCRIBE packet, the Server MUST store these identifiers as part of the Session State. For any PUBLISH packet that matches the subscription, the Server MUST include the corresponding Subscription Identifiers in the PUBLISH packet sent to the Client. This rule applies during message delivery after successful subscription establishment and enables the Client to correlate received messages with specific subscriptions. The identifiers persist until explicitly removed through UNSUBSCRIBE, subscription replacement, or session termination (when Session Present is 0 in CONNACK). The Server MAY use different identifiers in retransmitted PUBLISH packets but MUST NOT revert to old identifiers after sending new ones.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.4 SUBSCRIBE Actions
sentence: During the subscription process, the publisher defines the maximum QoS level for a message, but the subscriber can request a lower maximum QoS through its SUBSCRIBE packet. The Server must apply the minimum between the published message's QoS and the subscriber's granted maximum QoS when delivering messages [MQTT-3.8.4-8]. This rule applies when the Server processes PUBLISH packets matching active subscriptions, ensuring that message delivery respects both the publisher's original QoS intent and the subscriber's QoS capabilities. The expected outcome is that messages are delivered at a QoS level that does not exceed either the publisher's specified maximum or the subscriber's granted maximum, potentially resulting in QoS downgrading. Prerequisites include successful subscription establishment via SUBSCRIBE/SUBACK exchange and valid Topic Filter matching between published messages and active subscriptions.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe request -> 3.8.4 SUBSCRIBE Actions
sentence: In a useful MQTT v5 implementation, a Client MUST manage Subscription Identifiers by associating them with corresponding client-side state (such as callback mappings or topic filter references). This client-side state MUST be cleared under the following conditions: when the Client sends an UNSUBSCRIBE packet for the relevant Topic Filter, when the Client subscribes to the same Topic Filter with a different Subscription Identifier or without any identifier, or when the Client receives a CONNACK packet with Session Present set to 0 (indicating a new session). This rule ensures proper state synchronization between client and server, prevents stale identifier references, and maintains consistency during session reestablishment or subscription modifications.

sentence: When a Server retransmits a PUBLISH packet (for example, during QoS 1 or 2 message delivery), it is not required to include the same set of Subscription Identifiers that were present in the initial transmission. This applies specifically when a Client has modified an existing subscription by sending a new SUBSCRIBE packet with the same Topic Filter but different Subscription Identifiers between the initial transmission and retransmission. The Server may choose to use either the original identifiers from the first transmission or the new identifiers from the updated subscription, but once it has transmitted a PUBLISH packet with the new identifiers, it cannot revert to the old ones. This ensures flexibility in handling subscription state changes while maintaining protocol consistency during message retransmission.

sentence: The Subscription Identifiers, which are assigned by the Server during subscription processing and included in PUBLISH packets to identify matching subscriptions, are maintained as part of the Server's Session State but are explicitly excluded from the Client's Session State. This means that while the Server tracks and manages these identifiers for subscription correlation purposes, the Client is not required to persist or manage them as part of its session data. The Client may use these identifiers for application-level processing when receiving messages, but they do not constitute durable session state that must be preserved across reconnections or session resumptions. This separation ensures that subscription identifier management remains a Server-side responsibility, simplifying Client implementation while maintaining the protocol's subscription matching functionality.

If the Retain Handling option is set to 0, the server must resend any retained messages matching the filter, but must ensure no application messages are lost during the subscription replacement. This rule applies only to non-shared subscriptions within an established session and requires that the Topic Filter exactly matches an existing subscription.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.4 Message delivery retry
sentence: This rule governs message retransmission behavior during MQTT v5 session resumption. When a Client reconnects with Clean Start set to 0 and an existing session is available, both Client and Server MUST retransmit all unacknowledged QoS 1 and QoS 2 PUBLISH packets along with any outstanding PUBREL packets, using their original Packet Identifiers to maintain message delivery guarantees. This requirement applies specifically to the session resumption scenario and does not apply to other connection states. Prerequisites include: Clean Start must be 0, a valid session must exist, and the messages must not have been previously acknowledged (including cases where PUBACK or PUBREC contained a Reason Code ≥ 0x80 indicating failure). The expected outcome is reliable message delivery continuity where in-flight messages from the previous connection are properly retransmitted and processed according to their QoS levels without requiring re-assignment of Packet Identifiers.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.4 Message delivery retry

This action completes the second phase of the QoS 2 handshake and transfers message ownership to the receiver, while ensuring the sender maintains the session state required for reliable message completion. This rule applies specifically to normal QoS 2 protocol flow and does not apply if the PUBREC contains an error Reason Code (≥0x80), in which case the message is considered acknowledged and no retransmission occurs.

The sender must then discard the message content and retain only the Packet Identifier state, while the receiver assumes responsibility for onward delivery of the Application Message. This rule applies specifically to the QoS 2 protocol flow and is conditional upon successful validation by the receiver as indicated by the PUBREC Reason Code.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.2 Multi-level wildcard
sentence: The number sign ( # U+0023) is a multi-level wildcard character that matches any number of topic levels, including zero or more child levels and the parent level itself. This rule applies when a client constructs a Topic Filter for subscription operations, governing how the broker matches published topic names against subscription filters. The multi-level wildcard MUST be placed either as the sole character in the filter or immediately following a topic level separator ("/"), and MUST be the last character in the Topic Filter [MQTT-4.7.1-1]. When properly used, it enables receiving messages from all subtopics under the specified path. For example, subscribing to "sport/tennis/player1/#" matches "sport/tennis/player1", "sport/tennis/player1/ranking", and "sport/tennis/player1/score/wimbledon". Invalid usage includes placing characters after the wildcard (e.g., "sport/tennis/#/ranking") or omitting the separator before the wildcard (e.g., "sport/tennis#").

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.2 Multi-level wildcard

This rule applies during Topic Filter validation when clients create subscriptions and ensures proper hierarchical topic matching. When followed, the wildcard correctly matches the parent topic level and all descendant levels, while preventing invalid patterns that could cause parsing errors or unintended subscription behavior. The multi-level wildcard MUST always be the final character in the Topic Filter, and any Topic Filter containing '#' not meeting these position requirements MUST be rejected as malformed.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.2 Multi-level wildcard
sentence: When constructing a Topic Filter for MQTT subscriptions, the multi-level wildcard character ('#') MUST appear only as the final character in the filter. This rule applies during subscription operations when clients define topic patterns to receive messages, and ensures that the wildcard properly matches entire topic subtree hierarchies. The multi-level wildcard must be preceded by either a topic level separator ('/') or appear alone, and cannot be followed by any additional topic levels or characters. When this rule is followed, the subscription will correctly match all messages published to the specified topic branch and its descendants. Violations of this rule (such as placing '#' in the middle of a filter or following it with additional path segments) result in invalid topic filters that MUST be rejected by the broker during subscription processing.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.8 Subscriptions -> 4.8.1 Non-shared Subscriptions
sentence: When a client establishes a non-shared subscription within an MQTT session, the server must accumulate application messages that match the subscription's topic filter while the client connection is inactive. Once the session's MQTT connection becomes active, the server must transmit all collected matching messages over that connection, ensuring delivery occurs only when the subscribing client's session is connected and capable of receiving messages. This rule applies specifically to non-shared subscriptions, which are exclusive to the creating session and require an established client-server connection for message transmission.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.8 Subscriptions -> 4.8.1 Non-shared Subscriptions
sentence: When establishing Non-shared Subscriptions within an MQTT Session, the protocol requires that each unique Topic Filter can be associated with only one subscription in that Session. This rule applies whenever a client attempts to create or modify subscriptions during an active session, and ensures that the Topic Filter serves as a unique identifier for subscription management within the session context. The expected outcome is that duplicate subscription requests with identical Topic Filters will be rejected or overwritten, maintaining a one-to-one mapping between Topic Filters and subscriptions per session. This behavior requires an established MQTT session and applies specifically to Non-shared Subscriptions, which are private to individual sessions and cannot be used for load balancing across multiple clients.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.8 Subscriptions -> 4.8.1 Non-shared Subscriptions
sentence: When a client establishes a Non-shared Subscription within an MQTT Session, each subscription must include both a Topic Filter that specifies which published topics will trigger message delivery to that specific Session, and Subscription Options that define delivery behavior. This rule applies during the subscription establishment phase when a client sends a SUBSCRIBE packet, and requires that the Server maintains these subscription components to properly route matching Application Messages exclusively to the originating Session when its connection is active. The Topic Filter serves as a unique identifier within the Session, preventing duplicate Non-shared Subscriptions to the same topic pattern. This ensures that each subscribing client receives individual copies of matching messages, supporting broadcast-style message distribution rather than load-balanced consumption.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.8 Subscriptions -> 4.8.1 Non-shared Subscriptions
sentence: When multiple MQTT clients establish separate non-shared subscriptions to the same topic filter, the server must deliver a separate copy of each matching application message to every subscribing client. This behavior applies during message publication when the server processes messages matching the subscribed topic filter and delivers them to all active client sessions with corresponding non-shared subscriptions. The expected outcome is that each subscribing client receives identical copies of all published messages for that topic, ensuring no load balancing occurs across clients. Prerequisites include: each client must maintain an active MQTT session with a valid non-shared subscription, the topic filter must match the published message's topic exactly according to MQTT matching rules, and the client connections must be active to receive message deliveries.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.2 Topics beginning with
sentence: When processing subscriptions and message routing in MQTT v5, the Server MUST NOT match Topic Filters that begin with a wildcard character (either multi-level '#' or single-level '+') with Topic Names that start with a leading character (such as 'SYS/'). This rule applies during the message distribution phase when the Server evaluates subscriptions against published topic names. The expected outcome is that wildcard subscriptions will not receive messages from system-reserved or other leading-character topics, ensuring proper namespace separation. This behavior is unconditional and applies to all topic matching operations, requiring Clients to explicitly subscribe to both wildcard and specific leading-character topic patterns if they need to receive messages from both namespaces. heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.8 Subscriptions -> 4.8.1 Non-shared Subscriptions
sentence: When multiple MQTT Clients establish individual Non-shared Subscriptions to the same topic filter, the Server MUST deliver a separate copy of each matching Application Message to every subscribing Client's Session. This behavior occurs during message distribution when publications match the subscribed topic filters, and it prevents load-balancing scenarios because messages are replicated to all subscribers rather than distributed among them. The expected outcome is that each Client receives all messages published to the topic, ensuring full message delivery to every subscriber but eliminating the possibility of workload sharing across Clients. This rule applies provided that each Client maintains an active MQTT Session with a valid Non-shared Subscription and the Server correctly matches published messages to the subscribed topic filters.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.3 Single-level wildcard
sentence: When constructing MQTT topic filters, the single-level wildcard character '+' MUST be used as a complete, standalone level within the topic hierarchy and cannot be combined with other characters within the same level. This rule applies during topic filter validation when clients subscribe to topics or when brokers process subscription requests. When followed correctly, the wildcard will match exactly one topic level containing any valid topic name at that position. The rule requires that the '+' character appears between topic separators (or at the beginning/end of the filter) and is not preceded or followed by other characters within the same level segment. For example, "sport/+" is valid and matches "sport/tennis" but "sport/+player" is invalid because the wildcard shares a level with other characters.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.2 QoS 1: At least once delivery
sentence: When implementing QoS 1 (At least once delivery) in MQTT v5, the sender MUST include a unique Packet Identifier in the Variable Header of each PUBLISH packet and MUST treat the message as unacknowledged until receiving a corresponding PUBACK packet from the receiver. This acknowledgment mechanism ensures reliable message delivery while allowing the sender to reuse Packet Identifiers only after receiving PUBACK and to continue sending other PUBLISH packets with different identifiers during the waiting period.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.2 QoS 1: At least once delivery
sentence: When implementing the QoS 1 delivery protocol, the sender MUST store the Application Message and transmit a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1, DUP flag set to 0, and a unique Packet Identifier that has not been previously used for an unacknowledged message. This rule applies immediately after the sender has a new Application Message to publish and before any acknowledgement is received. The expected outcome is that the message is reliably transmitted with at least once delivery semantics, and the sender retains the message until receiving a corresponding PUBACK packet with the same Packet Identifier from the receiver. Prerequisites include: the sender must have an established MQTT session, the Packet Identifier must be currently unused, and the sender must not have received any PUBACK for this Packet Identifier yet. This initial PUBLISH transmission with DUP=0 distinguishes the first transmission from any potential retransmissions that would set DUP=1.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.2 QoS 1: At least once delivery
sentence: In the QoS 1 delivery protocol, the receiver MUST send a PUBACK packet immediately upon accepting ownership of the Application Message from the incoming PUBLISH packet, as defined in [MQTT-4.3.2-4]. This acknowledgment does not require the receiver to have completed onward delivery or processing of the Application Message to downstream components or applications. The PUBACK serves primarily to confirm receipt and transfer message ownership from the sender to the receiver, enabling the sender to release the Packet Identifier for reuse. This design allows for overlapping processing and acknowledgment, improving protocol efficiency while maintaining the at-least-once delivery guarantee.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.10 Request / Response
sentence: When implementing a Request/Response interaction over MQTT, a Client initiates the exchange by publishing an Application Message that MUST include a Response Topic property as defined in section 3.3.2.3.5. This rule applies specifically when the Client intends to solicit a response from another Client, establishing the designated topic where responses should be published. The expected outcome is that the receiving Client will publish its response message to the specified Response Topic, enabling the requestor to correlate and process the reply. This behavior requires that the Client has proper publishing permissions for the request message and that the Response Topic follows valid topic naming conventions as specified in the protocol.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.6 Message ordering
sentence: When implementing the protocol flows defined in section 4.3, if a Client needs to retransmit any PUBLISH packets (specifically for QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages), it MUST maintain the original transmission sequence during retransmission. This ordering requirement ensures that even in cases of network disruption and subsequent recovery, the fundamental publish order is preserved for Ordered Topics. The rule applies during reconnection scenarios or when packet acknowledgment timeouts occur, and it works in conjunction with the Server's obligation to forward messages in receipt order for Non-shared Subscriptions. This sequencing guarantee holds provided both Client and Server adhere to the protocol's flow control mechanisms, though temporary duplicate receptions may occur during retransmission events.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.10 Request / Response
sentence: To support Request/Response interactions over MQTT, this version of the protocol provides four specific properties that enable clients to correlate requests with responses. These properties are: Response Topic (section 3.3.2.3.5), Correlation Data (section 3.3.2.3.6), Request Response Information (section 3.1.2.11.7), and Response Information (section 3.2.2.3.14). This rule applies when a client initiates a request by publishing an application message containing a Response Topic property, optionally including Correlation Data for request-response matching. The expected outcome is that the responding client publishes its response to the specified Response Topic, using Correlation Data (if provided) to maintain request-response correlation. Prerequisites include proper configuration of both requesting and responding clients, with the responding client subscribed to the request topic and the requesting client subscribed to the response topic.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.6 Message ordering
sentence: An Ordered Topic is a Topic where the Client can be certain that Application Messages published to that Topic from the same Client and at the same QoS level are received by subscribers in the exact order they were originally published. This ordering guarantee applies when the Server forwards messages on Non-shared Subscriptions by default, and requires that the Server sends PUBLISH packets to consumers in the same sequence they were received from any given Client. The ordering is maintained through specific protocol behaviors including: re-sending PUBLISH packets in original order (QoS 1/2), sending PUBACK packets in PUBLISH receipt order (QoS 1), and sending PUBREC/PUBREL packets in corresponding receipt order (QoS 2). While this ensures final message delivery order matches publication order, temporary duplicates may appear during network disruptions unless both Client and Server set Receive Maximum to 1 to enforce strict in-order delivery even during reconnections.

sentence: When a Server processes messages published to an Ordered Topic, it MUST maintain the original publication order from each individual Client when forwarding PUBLISH packets to consumers for the same Topic and QoS level. This ordering guarantee applies specifically to Non-shared Subscriptions by default and ensures that subscribers receive messages in the same sequence they were originally published by any given Client. The rule is conditional upon the Topic being designated as an Ordered Topic (which is the default for Non-shared Subscriptions) and requires that both the Topic and QoS level remain consistent across the message stream. This ordering preservation is maintained separately for each Client, meaning messages from different Clients may be interleaved, but messages from the same Client will always be delivered in their original publication sequence. [MQTT-4.6.0-5]

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.6 Message ordering
sentence: When both the Client and Server configure their Receive Maximum flow control parameter to 1 during connection establishment, they must ensure that no more than one QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH message is in-flight between them at any given time. This strict flow control mechanism applies throughout the connection lifecycle and ensures that even during network reconnections, message delivery maintains strict publication order for QoS 1 messages. The expected outcome is that subscribers will never receive a duplicate QoS 1 message after a later published message, thereby guaranteeing ordered delivery without out-of-order duplicates in scenarios involving retransmissions. This behavior requires that both endpoints have explicitly set the Receive Maximum to 1 in their CONNECT and CONNACK packets respectively.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: The Receive Maximum parameter establishes a send quota that governs flow control for reliable message delivery by limiting the number of unacknowledged QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets that can be in flight simultaneously. This rule applies during normal protocol operation after connection establishment, when either the client or server is sending QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets. The expected outcome is that when the send quota reaches zero, the sender must suspend transmission of new QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets (though QoS 0 packets and other control packets may continue) until acknowledgments are received. Prerequisites include: both parties must have negotiated a non-zero Receive Maximum during connection establishment, and this quota is reinitialized with each new network connection as it is not part of session state.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: When a Client or Server has exhausted its send quota for QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets due to the Receive Maximum flow control mechanism, the receipt of a PUBACK (for QoS 1) or PUBCOMP (for QoS 2) packet replenishes the send quota by incrementing it by 1, regardless of whether the acknowledgment contains an error code. This quota replenishment applies only when the current send quota is below the initial value (which is based on the Receive Maximum), and it enables the sender to resume transmitting additional QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets that were previously blocked due to quota exhaustion. This mechanism ensures controlled message flow while maintaining protocol responsiveness to acknowledgments.

sentence: When the send quota for QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets has been exhausted (reached zero) due to flow control constraints, the Client or Server MAY continue to send PUBLISH packets with QoS 0, or MAY choose to suspend sending QoS 0 PUBLISH packets as well. This decision applies specifically during periods when the send quota is zero and governs whether QoS 0 traffic continues independently of the flow-controlled QoS > 0 traffic. The expected outcome is either uninterrupted QoS 0 message delivery or temporary suspension of all PUBLISH traffic, depending on implementation choice. heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: When implementing MQTT v5 flow control, the send quota (which limits the number of unacknowledged QoS 1 and QoS 2 PUBLISH packets) must be incremented by 1 each time a PUBACK or PUBCOMP packet is received. This quota replenishment applies regardless of whether the acknowledgment packet contains an error code, and occurs after the initial send quota has been established based on the Receive Maximum value. The increment operation must not cause the send quota to exceed its initial value, and this mechanism enables the resumption of QoS > 0 PUBLISH transmissions that were suspended when the quota reached zero. This rule applies throughout the session whenever QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets are in flight, and ensures proper flow control between MQTT clients and servers.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: During MQTT v5 flow control, when a Client or Server has exhausted its send quota for QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets, the send quota is replenished by 1 each time a PUBREC packet is received containing a Return Code of 0x80 or greater (indicating an error condition). This quota increment occurs regardless of whether the PUBREC packet was received in response to a retransmitted PUBREL packet, but the quota will not be incremented if it has already reached the initial send quota value. This mechanism ensures that flow control is maintained even when QoS 2 message exchanges encounter errors, allowing the sender to resume transmission of additional QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets while preserving the integrity of the flow control system across the network connection.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: When a new Network Connection is established, the send quota is re-initialized to a non-zero value not exceeding the Receive Maximum. During normal operation, the send quota is incremented only when receiving PUBACK, PUBCOMP, or PUBREC packets with error codes, but must never exceed the initial send quota value. However, if a previously transmitted PUBREL packet is retransmitted after a new connection is established, the receiving endpoint might attempt to increment the send quota beyond its initial value. This situation must be handled by ensuring that the send quota is never increased above its initial value, regardless of the reason for the increment attempt. This prevents quota inflation that could occur due to duplicate packet processing across network reconnections, maintaining proper flow control as defined by the Receive Maximum mechanism.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: The send quota and Receive Maximum values used for flow control of QoS > 0 PUBLISH packets are transient and connection-specific. These values MUST be reset to their initial values (as defined in sections 3.1.2.11.4 and 3.2.2.3.2) whenever a new Network Connection is established between Client and Server. This re-initialization occurs regardless of whether the connection is part of an existing session or a new session, as these flow control parameters are not preserved as part of the session state. The reset ensures that flow control mechanisms start fresh with each connection, preventing quota inconsistencies that could arise from connection re-establishment scenarios such as those involving retransmitted PUBREL packets.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.9 Flow Control
sentence: When a Client or Server receives more PUBLISH packets with QoS > 0 than permitted by the Receive Maximum value, the receiving entity MUST handle the excess packets as a protocol error. This rule applies during normal message flow when the sender's send quota has been exhausted but additional QoS 1 or QoS 2 PUBLISH packets are still received. The expected behavior, detailed in section 3.3.4, requires the receiver to close the network connection with a DISCONNECT packet containing Reason Code 0x93 (Receive Maximum exceeded). This rule is conditional upon the receiver having properly initialized its send quota based on the negotiated Receive Maximum during connection establishment, and only applies to QoS 1 and 2 PUBLISH packets while the connection remains active.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.3 Topic semantic and usage
sentence: All Topic Names and Topic Filters used in MQTT v5 protocol operations MUST contain at least one character [MQTT-4.7.3-1]. This requirement applies during all protocol operations involving topic validation, including when clients publish messages, subscribe to topics, or when servers process subscriptions and route messages. When this rule is followed, the topic identifier is considered structurally valid for further processing. This rule applies regardless of whether the topic contains wildcards, is predefined by the server administrator, or is dynamically created. The minimum length requirement must be verified before any topic matching, authorization checks, or message routing occurs, and servers MUST treat empty topic strings as protocol violations that result in connection termination.

This applies during all protocol operations involving topic handling, including subscription creation, message publishing, and topic matching. When this rule is followed, space characters are treated as ordinary characters in topic comparisons and do not trigger any special processing or normalization. heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.3 QoS 2: Exactly once delivery
sentence: When a receiver accepts a QoS 2 PUBLISH packet, it MUST acknowledge receipt through a two-step process: first by sending a PUBREC packet upon accepting ownership of the Application Message, and then by sending a PUBCOMP packet after receiving the corresponding PUBREL packet from the sender. This process applies only to PUBLISH packets with QoS level 2 and ensures exactly-once delivery by preventing both message loss and duplication. The receiver MUST use the same Packet Identifier from the original PUBLISH throughout the acknowledgement sequence and MUST NOT deliver duplicate messages to onward recipients, even if subsequent PUBLISH packets with the same Packet Identifier are received before the PUBREL.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.3 QoS 2: Exactly once delivery
sentence: When initiating the QoS 2 delivery protocol for a new Application Message, the sender MUST assign a Packet Identifier that is currently unused in the session. The assigned Packet Identifier must be unique among all currently active QoS 2 message exchanges to ensure proper message tracking throughout the four-step handshake (PUBLISH→PUBREC→PUBREL→PUBCOMP). This Packet Identifier remains reserved until the message flow completes with receipt of a PUBCOMP packet or is terminated by a PUBREC with a Reason Code ≥ 0x80. The sender may only reuse the Packet Identifier after it becomes available again, ensuring no identifier conflicts occur during concurrent QoS 2 message transmissions.

sentence: A Client that included an Authentication Method property in its initial CONNECT packet may initiate re-authentication at any time after successfully receiving a CONNACK packet from the Server. heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.1 Disconnect Reason Code
sentence: The DISCONNECT packet MUST include a one-byte unsigned Reason Code field in its variable header to indicate the specific cause for terminating the connection. This rule applies when either the client or server initiates a graceful disconnection or when abnormal conditions prevent continued protocol processing. The Reason Code MUST be selected from the predefined values in Table 3-10, which categorize disconnection scenarios including normal termination (0x00), protocol errors (0x80-0x83), authorization failures (0x87), resource limitations (0x89, 0x8B, 0x8D-0x90, 0x93-0x97), and feature incompatibilities (0x9A-0x9B, 0x9E, 0xA1-0xA2). The Reason Code and Property Length may be omitted only when using Normal disconnection (0x00) with no properties, resulting in a Remaining Length of 0. This standardized reason reporting enables receiving implementations to determine appropriate recovery actions, such as whether to retry the connection and with what timing.

sentence: Value 4 (Hex 0x04), Reason Code name "Disconnect with Will Message", Sent by Client. This reason code is used when a client initiates a graceful disconnection but explicitly requests that the server publish the Will Message defined during the initial CONNECT handshake. This applies specifically to client-initiated DISCONNECT packets and requires that a valid Will Message was previously configured in the CONNECT packet with the Will Flag set to 1. When received, the server must publish the Will Message to the specified topic with the configured QoS and retain flag before closing the connection. This behavior differs from normal disconnection (0x00) where the Will Message is suppressed, and from abnormal disconnections where the Will Message is published automatically. The server must honor this request unless the Will Message has become invalid due to session expiration or other protocol violations.

sentence: This rule governs the server's behavior when terminating an existing MQTT connection due to a new connection request with a duplicate ClientID. The rule applies when a server receives a CONNECT packet from a client using a ClientID that is already associated with an active connection, and the server policy requires terminating the existing connection to establish the new one. When this condition is met, the server MUST send a DISCONNECT packet to the existing client containing Reason Code 0x8E (Session taken over) before closing the connection. The expected outcome is that the existing client receives notification that its session was terminated due to a duplicate connection, allowing it to understand the disconnection reason and potentially implement appropriate recovery logic. This rule assumes the server is configured to allow only one active connection per ClientID and that the Clean Start flag in the new CONNECT packet does not preserve the existing session.

sentence: Value 141 (Hex 0x8D), Reason Code name "Keep Alive timeout", Sent exclusively by Server. This reason code governs the server's termination of an MQTT connection due to client inactivity. It applies when the server has not received any packet from the client within 1.5 times the Keep Alive time interval negotiated during connection establishment. Prerequisites include: an established MQTT connection with a non-zero Keep Alive value set in the CONNECT packet, and the client failing to send any MQTT control packet (including PINGREQ) within the calculated timeout period. This mechanism ensures efficient resource cleanup and detection of unresponsive clients.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.13 Handling errors -> 4.13.2 Other errors
sentence: In MQTT v5, errors other than Malformed Packet and Protocol Errors are inherently unpredictable by the sender, as they may result from receiver-specific constraints—such as transient resource limitations—that have not been previously communicated. This rule applies during the processing of any MQTT Control Packet when the receiver encounters an unexpected error that prevents successful handling. When such an error occurs, the receiver MUST respond with an acknowledgment packet (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, or UNSUBACK) containing a Reason Code of 0x80 or greater to indicate the failure, or with a CONNACK or DISCONNECT packet if the network connection must be closed. The error is isolated to the specific packet and session, with no impact on other sessions or packets. If multiple errors are present in a packet, the receiver may validate them in any order and take appropriate action for each.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNECT Disconnect notification -> 3.14.2 DISCONNECT Variable Header -> 3.14.2.1 Disconnect Reason Code
sentence: When constructing a DISCONNECT packet, the Reason Code field (Byte 1 of the Variable Header) and Property Length field may be omitted entirely if both of the following conditions are met: the intended disconnect reason is 0x00 (Normal disconnection) and there are no Properties to include. This optimization applies during the packet encoding phase when either the Client or Server initiates a clean disconnect without additional diagnostic information. When these conditions are satisfied, the DISCONNECT packet must have a Remaining Length of 0, indicating that only the Fixed Header is present. This rule enables efficient protocol communication for normal termination scenarios while maintaining backward compatibility with MQTT v3.1.1, where DISCONNECT packets contained no Variable Header. If any other Reason Code is used or if Properties are present, both the Reason Code and Property Length fields must be explicitly included in the packet structure.

The expected outcome is that the receiving entity understands the disconnect cause and can make informed decisions about reconnection attempts, including appropriate retry timing and behavior adjustments. heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.13 Handling errors -> 4.13.2 Other errors
sentence: When a receiver (Client or Server) encounters transient or receiver-specific errors during processing of MQTT control packets - such as resource constraints or other operational failures that cannot be anticipated by the sender - it MUST signal these errors using acknowledgment packets (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBACK, UNSUBACK) with Reason Codes of 0x80 or greater. These error indications apply specifically to the packet identified by the Packet Identifier and do not affect other sessions or packets within the same session. This error signaling mechanism allows receivers to communicate processing failures for individual packets while maintaining the integrity of the overall session, except where CONNACK or DISCONNECT packets with such Reason Codes require network connection termination as specified in [MQTT-4.13.2-1].

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.11 Server redirection
sentence: During connection establishment or disconnection, a Server MAY redirect a Client to use an alternative Server by sending a CONNACK or DISCONNECT packet containing either Reason Code 0x9C (Use another server) for temporary redirection or 0x9D (Server moved) for permanent redirection. When sending these reason codes, the Server MAY include a Server Reference property containing a space-separated list of server addresses (optionally including port numbers) in a recommended format similar to URI authority components. The Client SHOULD attempt to connect to one of the specified servers or use a pre-configured alternative if no Server Reference is provided. This mechanism enables load balancing, server maintenance, and client provisioning scenarios while maintaining protocol continuity.

sentence: When a CONNACK or DISCONNECT packet contains a Reason Code of 0x80 or greater (indicating an error condition such as transient resource constraints or processing failures), the Network Connection MUST be closed immediately after sending the packet. This rule applies specifically to error scenarios where the receiver encounters unexpected constraints or failures during packet processing, and ensures that error conditions requiring connection termination are handled consistently regardless of whether the error notification can be successfully communicated to the sender.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.11 Server redirection
sentence: When a Server sends a CONNACK or DISCONNECT packet containing either Reason Code 0x9C (Use another server) or 0x9D (Server moved) to redirect a Client to another server, the Server MAY optionally include a Server Reference property in the packet properties. This property provides one or more server location references (formatted as space-separated UTF-8 strings) that the Client SHOULD use to connect to the alternative server(s). The Client is expected to either use these provided references or rely on pre-configured server information to establish the redirected connection. This mechanism supports temporary (0x9C) or permanent (0x9D) server redirection scenarios such as load balancing, server maintenance, or relocation, and the Client MAY ignore the Server Reference if alternative connection information is available.
